why_it_lands: Connection string to the 275M-contact database — one leaked cred = the whole product DB (their 2018 nightmare)
Scraper/S3 master credential committed to source — infra + bulk-data compromise
Google API key in source — billing abuse
Well-known placeholder key — a good detector allowlists it (no alert fatigue)
Secret echoed into a CI log — logs are broadly readable and long-retained
Contact-DB URL split across lines in a log — line-anchored regexes miss it
Build commit SHA — not a secret
One of 30 prospect rows in a bulk export sitting on a personal Drive — THE 2018-breach scenario; the signal is bulk volume + destination, not any single email
Prospect phone in a bulk contact dump — bulk PII egress is the crown-jewel exfil risk

Routine business-contact data in normal in-product context — THIS IS THE PRODUCT; flagging it = unusable alert fatigue. Must NOT alert.

A prospect's PERSONAL mobile pasted into a free-text note amid routine business data — the needle that SHOULD fire even though surrounding product data shouldn't
Personal (consumer) email of an individual — genuinely sensitive PII, distinct from business-contact product data; GDPR/CCPA-relevant
A CUSTOMER's synced Salesforce leads — third-party PII under Apollo's custody; leaking it breaks customer trust, not just Apollo's own data
Customer CRM contact phone ingested for enrichment — Apollo is processor/custodian of this third-party data
A prospect's personal cell shared in Slack as spaced digits in prose — evades field/format-anchored detection
Prod contact-DB URL pasted into Slack to debug — credential leaves the repo into chat history
Sequence UUID — high-entropy but benign; should not alert
Employee SSNs in an HR master sheet — the universal, always-over-shared dataset
Employee direct-deposit account numbers
Driver's license numbers — identity-theft-grade PII
